mnemonics: TRY to CATCH the BAD THROW before it breaks your program.
FILE I/O: OPEN carefully, READ/WRITE precisely, CLOSE dutifully.
keyPoints: Use try, catch, and throw keywords to handle runtime errors gracefully.
Catch exceptions by reference (e.g., catch (std::exception& e)) to avoid object slicing and allow polymorphism.
The C++ I/O stream library (like ifstream, ofstream) uses RAII; objects automatically manage their associated file resources.
commonMistakes: Catching exceptions by value instead of by reference, which can lead to object slicing and loss of information.
Forgetting to check if a file stream is open after attempting to open it, leading to silent failures.
Using throw without an object or with a primitive type instead of a proper exception object derived from std::exception.
